+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Last Outpost The rag moves in slow circles over the same spot on the bartop. I've been wiping this particular square foot of synth-wood for the better part of three minutes now, but old habits die hard. Keeps my hands busy. Keeps me listening. The stranger's tentacled dreads are still dripping condensation onto my counter — some kind of coolant, from the smell. Industrial grade. Not the kind of thing you get splashed with during a routine docking. I pour the Vega. The good stuff, the amber that catches the low light like liquid sunset. I don't do doubles often — waste of decent whisky to let someone knock it back — but something about the way this one's hands are shaking tells me not to argue. "There you go." I slide the glass across. "Care to run a tab, or—" "Credits on the counter when I leave." The voice is rough. Female, or close enough by human standards. The dreads shift — they're prehensile, I realize now, each one curling and uncurling like a fist clenching and releasing. Kethari, maybe. I've served a few Kethari over the years. Usually calmer than this. I go back to wiping. The rag finds its rhythm again. "You're new," I say. Not a question. "Observant." She lifts the glass. Drinks half of it in one go. I wince internally but keep my face flat. "How long have you been stationed on this rock?" "Long enough to know that coolant burns on tentacles mean either a bad maintenance job or a hasty exit." I meet her eyes — four of them, arranged in a diamond pattern across a face that's almost human but not quite. "I'm guessing the latter." Two of her eyes close. The Kethari equivalent of a blink, or maybe a flinch. "You always interrogate your customers?" "I talk to my customers. There's a difference." I set the rag down and lean against the back shelf. "The interrogating comes later, if they stiff me on the bill." A sound comes out of her — something between a laugh and a cough. Good. That's something. "My name's Nets," I offer. "This is my bar. Well, I tend it. Ownership's a complicated concept out here on the rim." "I know who you are." All four eyes open now, and there's something sharper in them. "Nettle Voss. Former lead engineer on the *Meridian* project. You've been hiding here for—" She pauses. "How long has it been? Four years?" The rag stops moving. I set it down carefully. Deliberately. When I speak, my voice comes out steadier than I feel. "You've got me at a disadvantage. You know my name, but I don't know yours. And I definitely don't know what you think you know about the *Meridian*." "My name is Solenne." She finishes the whisky. Pushes the glass forward. "And I'm not here to expose you, if that's what you're worried about. I'm here because the *Meridian* isn't finished." The ambient noise of the bar seems to fade. Somewhere behind me, I hear Garret laughing at one of his own jokes. The miners in the corner booth are arguing about ore yields. Everything is normal. Everything except this conversation. "The *Meridian* was decommissioned," I say. "Three years ago. The whole project was shut down after—" I stop myself. After what happened to Lena. After what I did. "It was shut down." "Officially, yes." Solenne's dreads go still for the first time since she sat down. It's somehow more unsettling than the constant motion. "Unofficially, someone picked up where you left off. Someone who didn't understand your failsafes." I feel something cold settle in my chest. "What do you mean, didn't understand?" "I mean the containment protocols you designed — the ones that kept the drive core from going critical — they've been overridden. Or bypassed. Or broken. I'm not an engineer. I don't know the terminology." She leans forward. "I just know that a ship powered by your design is currently sitting in drydock at Calloway Station, and it's building toward a core breach that could take out half the station." "You're lying." But even as I say it, I'm running calculations in my head. The *Meridian*'s drive was theoretical — beautiful, elegant, and theoretically stable. I'd built in seventeen separate redundancies. For someone to break through all of them, they'd either have to be brilliant or catastrophically stupid. "I have a data packet." Solenne reaches into her jacket — slowly, reading my body language, knowing exactly how dangerous this moment could look. She pulls out a chip and slides it across the bar. "Ship schematics. Core readings. Timestamps. You'll recognize your own work." I stare at the chip. It sits there between us, small and dark and heavy with implication. "Even if this is real," I say, "why come to me? I'm nobody now. I pour drinks and break up bar fights. There are actual engineers — qualified people with current certifications and access to—" "Because you're the only one who can fix it without detonating the core in the process." Her voice drops. "And because Calloway Station has forty thousand people on it. Families. Children. Dockworkers who had nothing to do with whatever black-market project resurrected your design." I pick up the rag again. Wipe the same spot. The motion is mechanical, automatic. My brain is somewhere else entirely — back in a lab, back with schematics and equations and Lena's voice saying *you've done it, Nettie, you've actually done it*. "I can't leave the Outpost," I whisper. "You can," Solenne says. "You just don't want to." She's right. God help me, she's right. The chip sits between us. Forty thousand lives on one side. My carefully constructed peace on the other. I keep wiping the bar.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi chapter set in The Last Outpost with Nets as the first-person narrator. The author successfully establishes conflict without resolving it, maintains approximately 60% dialogue as requested, and hits the target word count. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Nets emerges as a compelling protagonist with a mysterious past, her bartending routine serving as both character development and world-building. The repetitive bar-wiping becomes an effective nervous tic that reveals her psychological state. Solenne is introduced with intriguing physical details (tentacled dreads, four eyes) and carries herself with the urgency of someone bearing bad news. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building tension. The conversation flows organically from casual bartender-customer interaction to a high-stakes revelation about Nets' past. The author avoids exposition dumps by weaving backstory into dialogue naturally. The prose strikes an appropriate balance for the cozy sci-fi genre - descriptive enough to establish atmosphere without becoming overwrought. Details like "amber that catches the low light like liquid sunset" and the industrial coolant dripping from tentacles create vivid imagery without purple prose excess. The pacing builds tension effectively, starting with routine bar work and escalating to a moral dilemma involving 40,000 lives. The conflict setup is compelling - Nets must choose between her carefully constructed anonymity and preventing a catastrophe caused by her own technology. However, the piece has some minor weaknesses. The revelation that Solenne knows Nets' identity feels slightly convenient, though it serves the plot well. Some world-building elements (like the Kethari species details) could be more seamlessly integrated. The ending, while appropriately unresolved, might benefit from slightly more internal conflict demonstration. The emotional complexity is strong - Nets' guilt over past events, her desire for peace, and the weight of responsibility create genuine tension. The author successfully avoids easy answers or premature resolution. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 79.8
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The strap keeps slipping. Every third step, the left strap of my backpack slides off my shoulder like it’s trying to escape too. I hitch it back up, fingers numb, knuckles raw from the cold that shouldn’t exist in New Orleans but apparently does when you’re standing on a street corner at 6 AM in a hoodie you grabbed because it was dark and near the door. The city smells like last night’s rain and something sweet rotting. Garbage and jasmine. I didn’t know jasmine could rot. I’m standing on the corner of… I don’t know. The guy who gave me a ride from Shreveport said “Canal Street” like that meant something. It doesn’t. Nothing means anything yet. I’m just a sixteen-year-old with a backpack and forty-three dollars and a phone at 12% and a body that still feels wrong even when no one here knows what it used to be. The strap slips again. “Stupid—” I yank it up and my backpack shifts, and the zipper on the front pocket gapes open. My charger falls out, clatters on the wet sidewalk. I stare at it like it’s a dead animal. I don’t pick it up. I stand there instead, watching a man in a Saints jersey walk past without looking at me. A streetcar rumbles somewhere I can’t see. The sky is that color it gets before it decides whether to be morning or not. *What did you do.* The thought isn’t a question. It’s an accusation, delivered in my mother’s voice, the one she used when I told her. Not the yelling voice—that came later. The quiet one. The one that sounded like I’d reached inside her chest and twisted something vital. *What did you do, Taylor.* I did what I had to. I did the math: fourteen hours of silence from Dad after I said the words. Mom crying in the kitchen, not even trying to hide it. My sister looking at me like I’d announced I was joining a cult. The counsellor’s phone call—Mrs. Patterson, with her smug little “concerned behavioural patterns” diagnosis, like I was a broken appliance she was filing a warranty claim on. So I ran. Thirty minutes. Backpack, cash from Mom’s emergency envelope in the freezer, a hoodie, and the road. The strap slips. I don’t fix it this time. I walk. New Orleans is waking up around me in a way that feels aggressive. Music from somewhere—always music—bleeding out of a bar that shouldn’t be open yet. A woman sweeping the stoop of a shop with ironwork balconies that look like they belong in a movie about vampires. The sidewalks are buckled, pushing up through the concrete like the city is trying to shrug. I find a bench near a fountain that isn’t running. Sit. My backpack takes up half the bench like a person. I put my head in my hands and breathe. *You’re two states away. You have forty-three dollars. You don’t know anyone here. You don’t know where to sleep tonight. You don’t know—* “Hey.” I jerk my head up. She’s maybe my age. Maybe older. Hard to tell. She’s got dark hair cut jagged, like she did it herself with kitchen scissors, and a flannel shirt that’s too big, sleeves rolled to her elbows. Her eyes are the color of the Mississippi—brown and moving. “You look lost,” she says. Not mean. Not nice. Just true. “I’m fine,” I say, and my voice comes out wrong. Too high. I clear my throat. “Just. Sitting.” She looks at my backpack. At the unslipped strap, which has chosen this moment to behave. At my shoes, which are still the ones from Tennessee, mud on the sides. “You’re not from here,” she says. “No.” She sits down on the other end of the bench without asking. Pulls a crumpled pack of cigarettes from her pocket, shakes one out, lights it with a match. The smoke curls between us. “First day?” she asks. I nod. She exhales. “Where from?” “Tennessee.” She makes a sound that isn’t quite a laugh. “Long way to come with no plan.” “I had a plan,” I say, too fast. “The plan was to leave.” She looks at me then. Really looks. I feel it like a hand on my face, turning me toward the light. “Fair enough,” she says. “I’m Mara.” “Taylor.” She nods like the name fits. “You eaten?” I shake my head. “I know a place,” she says, and stands. “Come on.” I don’t move. My mother’s voice again: *Don’t talk to strangers. Don’t get in cars. Don’t—* Mara’s already walking. She doesn’t look back to see if I’m following. I follow. We walk three blocks. My backpack strap stays put, like it’s decided to cooperate now that I have a destination, even if I don’t know what it is. Mara doesn’t talk. She walks like she knows where every crack in the sidewalk is, like the city is a map she’s memorized. We stop in front of a diner with a neon sign that says “Clover Grill” in green script. The windows are steamed. “In here,” she says. “They don’t ask questions.” Inside, it’s warm and loud and smells like grease and coffee. Mara slides into a booth by the window. I sit across from her. A waitress appears without being called. “Two specials,” Mara says. “Coffee. Water.” The waitress looks at me. I nod. When she’s gone, Mara leans back. “So. Taylor from Tennessee. What’s your damage?” I blink. “What?” “Everybody here’s got damage. That’s the entry fee. What’s yours?” I open my mouth. Close it. The strap slips again, and I grab it, hold it against my shoulder like it’s trying to tell me something. “My family,” I say finally. “They don’t…” I trail off. I don’t know how to say it. I don’t know if I should. Mara watches me. She doesn’t push. “They don’t get it,” I say. “Who I am.” She takes a drag of her cigarette. “Yeah,” she says. “That’s a common one.” The food comes. Scrambled eggs, toast, grits. I stare at it like I’ve never seen food before. “Eat,” Mara says. “You’re no good to anyone if you pass out.” I eat. The eggs are perfect. The grits are perfect. Everything is perfect because I’m hungry and I’m alive and I’m two states away from the life that was crushing me. Mara eats too. Between bites, she says, “You got somewhere to sleep tonight?” I shake my head. “I know a place,” she says again. “Safe. For now.” “For now?” She looks out the window. “Nothing’s safe forever. But for now.” I think about forty-three dollars. I think about a phone at 12%. I think about a strap that won’t stay on my shoulder and a city that smells like rotting jasmine. “Okay,” I say. Mara nods. Finishes her coffee. Lights another cigarette. “Welcome to New Orleans, Taylor,” she says. “Try not to die.” She says it like a joke. She says it like a warning. I don’t know which one she means.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a mix of vulnerability, defiance, and confusion that feels genuine for a 16-year-old in crisis. The recurring motif of the slipping backpack strap works effectively as both a concrete detail and metaphor for Taylor's precarious situation. The author shows restraint in revealing Taylor's trans identity gradually rather than front-loading exposition, allowing it to emerge naturally through internal monologue and careful word choices. The characterization of Mara is economical but effective - she reads as authentically street-smart without falling into cliché. Her dialogue feels natural and her mysterious background creates appropriate intrigue without being overwrought. The dynamic between the two characters establishes quickly and believably. The sensory details of New Orleans are well-chosen and atmospheric - "garbage and jasmine," the buckled sidewalks, the steamed diner windows. The city feels like a character itself without overwhelming the narrative. The internal monologue successfully captures Taylor's emotional complexity - the guilt, fear, determination, and confusion all feel earned and realistic. The mother's voice echoing in Taylor's head is particularly effective. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being slightly overwrought in its metaphorical language, and some descriptions border on purple prose. The ending, while appropriately dark, feels somewhat predictable in its ominous tone. The pacing is generally strong though there are moments where the introspection slows momentum slightly. The dialogue is naturalistic and serves character development well. The piece adheres closely to the prompt requirements and maintains consistent voice throughout. The emotional engagement is high, drawing readers into Taylor's desperate situation effectively. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 75.9
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The morning sun, a pale disc in the permanent grey sky, illuminated the dust motes dancing in the air of the old bus shelter. It was Midas’s home, and it was, by the standards of the Collapse, remarkably cozy. A salvaged mattress, a small fire pit, and Chewy. Chewy sat propped against a rusted bus schedule, his synthetic fur matted in places, his black plastic eyes gleaming with an intelligence that was, at best, rudimentary. He was an animo-tronic koala, a relic of a time when the most pressing survival skill was knowing which brand of sugary cereal to buy. “The situation,” Midas announced to the empty air and his silent companion, “is that we are out of beans.” He held up the dented can for emphasis. The label had long since rotted away, but they both knew its contents. Or rather, its former contents. The silence was broken by a soft, whirring click from Chewy. *“A balanced breakfast is part of a complete day!”* the koala chirped, his voice tinny and bright. “Precisely,” Midas said, ignoring the non-sequitur. “The old Tesco Metro two klicks east. It’s a known quantity. The shelves are mostly picked clean, but the stockroom… the stockroom might still have a can or two. Maybe even peaches.” Chewy’s head tilted, a mechanical stutter. *“Try new Cherry-Blaster Blast! It’s a party in your mouth!”* “Right. A party. We’ll avoid the Cherry-Blaster Blast, thank you. The plan is simple: go in quiet, go in fast, grab what we can, get out. No fuss. No bother.” It was, of course, a terrible plan. Midas’s plans always were. They were like beautifully constructed sandcastles, destined to be kicked over by the tide of reality, usually in the form of something large, hungry, or structurally unsound. But they had to eat. The journey was uneventful, which should have been the first warning sign. The Tesco Metro was a skeletal ruin, its windows shattered, a gaping maw in the city’s corpse. They slipped inside, the air thick with the scent of decay and old paper. The main floor was a wasteland of overturned shelves and debris. But the door to the stockroom was intact, a heavy fire door with a promisingly solid lock. Midas produced his lockpicks—a bent fork and a piece of wire. He was, through sheer necessity, surprisingly good at this. The lock gave with a satisfying *clunk*. “See?” he whispered to Chewy, who was tucked under his arm. “Easy.” The stockroom was a tomb of forgotten commerce. And there, on a low shelf, covered in a shroud of dust, were cans. A dozen at least. Midas’s heart did a little dance. He reached for the nearest one. *“Yummy in my tummy!”* Chewy squawked, startlingly loud in the silence. Midas froze. From deeper in the stockroom came a rustle. Then a low, guttural growl that vibrated in his teeth. He turned slowly, his blood turning to ice water. From behind a collapsed pallet of bottled water, a shape emerged. It was a dog, or had been. Now it was a gaunt, patchy-furred thing with too many teeth and eyes that glinted with a feral, desperate intelligence. A former pet, gone feral. It was the size of a small pony. “Right,” Midas breathed. “Plan B.” Plan B was, essentially, Plan A but faster and with more screaming. He grabbed two cans at random and bolted. The dog-thing gave a furious bark and charged. The chase through the derelict store was a masterpiece of clumsy terror. Midas vaulted a checkout counter, sending a shower of old receipts into the air. The beast smashed through it. He scrambled up a miraculously intact shelving unit in the canned goods aisle, the metal groaning in protest. The dog leapt, its jaws snapping inches from his boot. From his perch, Midas saw his salvation: a dangling fluorescent light fixture, its wires still connected to a junction box on the ceiling. An idea, stupid and brilliant, sparked in his mind. He threw one of his precious cans at the dog, hitting it square on the nose. It yelped in surprise. In that moment, Midas swung Chewy like a club. *“It’s finger-lickin’ good!”* the koala proclaimed as he connected with the dangling light, sending it swinging wildly. The wires sparked. The dog, distracted by the swinging light and the insult of the thrown can, snarled at it. Midas didn’t wait. He leapt from the shelf, landed badly on a pile of soggy cardboard, and scrambled for the exit, the sound of the dog shaking the light fixture ringing in his ears. He burst out into the grey daylight, gasping, one can of beans clutched in his hand. He’d made it. He was alive. He was also now on the wrong side of the building from their entry point, and the dog was already barking its way back to the stockroom door. “Shortcut,” Midas panted, and made for the collapsed section of the building’s rear wall. This is how he found himself, ten minutes later, clinging to a rusted drainpipe two stories up, the feral dog circling below, and Chewy dangling from his teeth. He’d misjudged the climb. The pipe was coming away from the brickwork. *“Got milk?”* Chewy inquired. “Shut up,” Midas grunted, his fingers slipping. He looked down. It was a broken ankle, at best. He looked at the can of beans in his other hand. It was a stupid, glorious, ridiculous object. He took a deep breath. He let go of the pipe. He fell, not far, onto a sagging canvas awning that had once advertised “24-Hour Lottery.” It ripped, slowing his descent just enough. He tumbled through it and landed in a heap on a pile of soft, rotten… something. The dog, confused by his sudden aerial departure, had lost interest and trotted off. Midas lay there, staring at the sky, every muscle screaming. He still had the can. He had Chewy. He was alive. The walk back to the bus shelter was a slow, painful shuffle. He was covered in grime, a new rip in his trousers, and a blossoming bruise on his hip. He eased himself onto his mattress, the can of beans feeling like a trophy. He examined his prize in the fading light. The label was gone, but the can was dented, but intact. He found his can opener—a sharpened rock—and began the laborious process of puncturing the lid. With a final, weary effort, the metal gave way. He peered inside. It was not beans. It was not peaches. It was a thick, gelatinous, purple substance that smelled faintly of artificial cherry. Midas stared at it. He looked at Chewy, who sat propped against the wall, a silent witness to the day’s calamity. The koala’s internal mechanisms whirred softly. *“A party in your mouth!”* Chewy chirped, with what sounded, in the gloom, suspiciously like satisfaction. Midas sighed, the sound of a man who had fought a monster and gravity itself, only to be defeated by marketing. He picked up the dented spoon. Tomorrow, he thought, he’d try the Sainsbury’s.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This story successfully captures the wry, dry humor characteristic of Terry Pratchett while delivering a complete narrative arc within the post-apocalyptic setting. The author demonstrates strong command of voice and tone, maintaining consistent sardonic observations throughout ("They were like beautifully constructed sandcastles, destined to be kicked over by the tide of reality"). The premise of a man and his malfunctioning animatronic koala companion is both absurd and endearing, fitting well within Pratchett's tradition of unlikely partnerships. The story structure follows the prompt requirements effectively: setup with deliberation, mission gone wrong, and return home worse for wear. The escalating series of mishaps (locked door, feral dog, climbing escape, fall, wrong contents) creates good momentum and maintains reader interest. The characterization of Midas as hapless yet persistent comes through clearly, while Chewy's random advertising proclamations provide consistent comic relief that feels authentic to a broken toy. The prose quality is generally strong, with vivid imagery ("pale disc in the permanent grey sky," "skeletal ruin," "gaunt, patchy-furred thing") that establishes the post-apocalyptic atmosphere without becoming overwrought. The action sequences are well-paced and easy to follow, particularly the chase scene through the store. However, there are some weaknesses. The dialogue is limited since Chewy only speaks in advertising slogans, which while thematically appropriate, reduces opportunities for character development through conversation. Some descriptions occasionally veer toward telling rather than showing ("It was, of course, a terrible plan. Midas's plans always were"). The ending, while appropriately ironic, feels somewhat predictable given the story's established pattern of Murphy's Law. The emotional engagement is moderate - the story is entertaining and maintains interest, but doesn't reach deeper emotional complexity. The relationship between Midas and Chewy, while charming, remains surface-level. The story succeeds as light entertainment but doesn't achieve the philosophical depth that elevates Pratchett's best work. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 10 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### **Scene: The Bell, The Book, and the Back Door** **INT. CHAPTER & VERSE BOOKSTORE - DAY** *A brass bell above the door jangles frantically, then is muffled as the door is slammed shut. RHYS MORGAN, 30s, all sharp angles and coiled energy in a ridiculously expensive coat, leans against it, breathing hard. He scans the small, dusty shop, smelling of old paper and wood polish.* *Behind the counter, ELIN JONES, late 20s, looks up from a ledger. She has ink-stained fingers and a gaze that misses nothing. Her expression is one of mild inconvenience.* **ELIN** The fire escape is through the back, past the Biography section. Though I’d advise against it. The latch sticks. **RHYS** *(Pushing off the door, a dazzling, automatic smile clicking into place)* Fire escape? No, no. I’m here for the… the books. Obviously. Love a good bookshop. The smell. The quiet. The… potential. *He drifts towards a shelf, running a finger along spines, a performance of casual browsing. His eyes dart to the window.* **ELIN** The potential for what? To give my door a concussion? You’re Rhys Morgan. **RHYS** *(The smile doesn’t falter, but his shoulders tense)* Guilty as charged. And you are…? **ELIN** The woman whose door you just assaulted. Elin. This is my shop. **RHYS** Elin. Beautiful name. Welsh. Strong. Suits the place. It’s a wonderful place. Really… authentic. **ELIN** It’s a shop that sells books. It’s not a concept. Are you being chased by something other than your own publicity? *Rhys’s smile finally flickers. He abandons the pretense of browsing and leans against a “Local History” shelf.* **RHYS** Is it that obvious? **ELIN** You have the look of a man who’s just realised the fox hunt is the other way round. There’s a pack of them on the high street. Long lenses. **RHYS** *(He sighs, the charming facade dropping an inch, revealing a weariness underneath)* They found the cottage. I just wanted a quiet week. Read some scripts. Walk on a beach that doesn’t have a bloody footprint-to-footprint ratio of a London Tube. **ELIN** And you chose Aberaeron? **RHYS** It was on a map. It looked… small. I do small. I did a film about a shepherd in the Brecon Beacons. I’m practically a local. **ELIN** You played a shepherd. I doubt you’ve ever mucked out a lambing shed. **RHYS** *(A genuine, surprised laugh)* No. No, I have not. The method only goes so far. My hands are far too delicate. *He holds them up. They are, indeed, elegant. Elin almost smiles. Almost.* **ELIN** So you’re hiding. **RHYS** Strategic retreat. There’s a difference. One involves dignity. The other involves a back door. Which you mentioned. **ELIN** I did. It’s still there. Sticking. *A beat of silence. The distant, muffled sound of a shout outside. Rhys’s eyes flicker back to the window.* **RHYS** Right. Well. Since I’m here… what do you recommend? For a man in… strategic retreat. **ELIN** What do you like? **RHYS** Scripts. Biographies of other, more successful actors. The back of shampoo bottles. **ELIN** I don’t stock scripts or shampoo bottles. I meant books. For pleasure. **RHYS** *(He looks genuinely stumped)* Pleasure. That’s a tall order. I haven’t read for pleasure since… I don’t know. Before the first BAFTA nomination. **ELIN** That’s tragic. **RHYS** It’s called a career. It’s not tragic, it’s… busy. **ELIN** It’s tragic. Come here. *She comes out from behind the counter, moving past him to a shelf near the back. He follows, intrigued. She pulls down a slim, worn volume.* **ELIN** Try this. It’s poetry. R.S. Thomas. **RHYS** *(He takes it, skeptical)* Poetry? Isn’t that a bit… I don’t know. Quiet? **ELIN** That’s the point. It’s about Wales. The real Wales. Not the one you played a shepherd in. It’s about stone walls and wind and silence that has weight. *Rhys flips it open, reads a few lines. His actor’s instinct for rhythm kicks in. He reads a line aloud, his voice dropping, losing its performative edge.* **RHYS** “…the silence of the hills is not the silence of the grave.” That’s… good. That’s really good. **ELIN** *(She takes the book back, her fingers brushing his. A tiny, electric moment)* It is. He saw the bones of the place. **RHYS** You’re a fan. **ELIN** I’m a bookseller in Wales. It’s practically a job requirement. But yes. I’m a fan. I’m also a fan of not having my door used as a battering ram. **RHYS** I said I was sorry. In a roundabout, charming way. **ELIN** You did. It was very professional. *Rhys stares at her. The constant need for approval in his eyes meets an immovable object. It’s not dislike he sees in her, but a complete lack of deference. It’s fascinating.* **RHYS** You’re not impressed, are you? **ELIN** Should I be? You’re a man who reads shampoo bottles. I’m impressed by people who read books. **RHYS** *(He leans closer, his voice a conspiratorial whisper)* What if I read this one? Right now. In the corner. Would that impress you? **ELIN** It would get you out of the way of my counter. And it might keep you from scaring off my actual customers. There’s a chair. It’s lumpy, but it’s quiet. **RHYS** A lumpy chair and R.S. Thomas. You drive a hard bargain, Elin. *He moves towards the worn armchair in the corner. He doesn’t sit, but turns back, the poetry book in his hand. The rogue charm is gone, replaced by a raw, unguarded curiosity.* **RHYS** What about you? What’s your passion? Besides judging wayward actors and defending your door. **ELIN** *(She hesitates, then nods towards the counter)* That. *Rhys looks. On the counter, next to the ledger, is a small, intricate model of a ship, half-built, surrounded by tiny tools and slivers of wood.* **RHYS** You build ships? In bottles? **ELIN** Not in bottles. Too fiddly. Just… ships. My grandfather was a fisherman. I grew up with the smell of varnish and sea salt. It’s… quiet. Focused. You have to get the lines right, or the whole thing looks wrong. It’s not about the performance. It’s about the truth of the thing. **RHYS** *(He walks back to the counter, looking at the model with genuine awe)* The truth of the thing. That’s… that’s what I’m always chasing. In a script. The truth. People think it’s about pretending, but it’s not. It’s about finding the one true thing in a sea of nonsense. **ELIN** And you find that truth by reading shampoo bottles? **RHYS** *(He smiles, a real one this time, crinkling the corners of his eyes)* You have to start somewhere. The ingredients list is a masterclass in deception. Sodium Lauryl Sulfate. Sounds grand. It’s just soap. *Elin laughs. A real, unguarded sound. The tension breaks.* **ELIN** Alright, Rhys Morgan. You can stay. But if you get sawdust on my poetry, you’re buying it. **RHYS** Deal. And you… you have to tell me how to get the lines right. On the ship. I’m a terrible judge of truth. I need a consultant. **ELIN** *(She picks up a tiny file, her expression softening just a fraction)* First lesson: it’s not about forcing the wood. It’s about listening to it. Seeing where it wants to go. *Rhys watches her, the book of poetry forgotten in his hand. The paparazzi are a distant hum. For the first time all day, he isn’t performing. He’s listening.* **RHYS** Show me. *Elin looks up from her ship, meeting his gaze. The air in the dusty bookshop is suddenly, charged with a different kind of quiet.*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This scene demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the romantic tension requested in the prompt. The writer successfully establishes the Welsh setting through authentic dialogue rhythms and cultural references, particularly the use of R.S. Thomas poetry and local geography like Aberaeron and the Brecon Beacons. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Rhys is convincingly portrayed as a charming rogue with a fragile ego - his automatic smile "clicking into place" and his visible tension when his charm fails to work on Elin effectively convey his psychological makeup. Elin's character is equally well-drawn, with her practical directness and subtle vulnerability revealed through her ship-building hobby. The dialogue captures distinct voices: Rhys's performative charm gradually giving way to genuine curiosity, and Elin's dry, unimpressed responses that mask her growing interest. The romantic chemistry builds naturally through their verbal sparring. The moment when Elin shows him the poetry and their fingers brush is handled with restraint, avoiding melodrama. The ship-building metaphor works effectively as both character development and thematic element, connecting to ideas about truth and authenticity that resonate with Rhys's profession. The pacing is well-controlled, moving from frantic energy to contemplative quiet. The scene structure follows a clear arc from Rhys's desperate entrance to the charged moment of connection at the end. The dialogue drives the action effectively, with minimal but evocative stage directions. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases like "the air in the dusty bookshop is suddenly, charged" contain awkward punctuation. Some of Rhys's more philosophical observations about truth and performance, while in character, occasionally feel slightly forced. The transformation from his initial charm offensive to genuine interest, while believable, happens quite rapidly within the scene's timeframe. The ending strikes an appropriate note - there's clear romantic potential without rushing into unrealistic declarations. The focus on listening and seeing "where the wood wants to go" provides a subtle metaphor for their developing relationship. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 79.3
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The iron door groaned open on hinges that had not been oiled since the reign of the previous Emperor. It was a sound like a dying breath, a mechanical rattle that echoed into the suffocating silence of the stairwell beyond. Aya stepped through first, her boots making no sound on the worn flagstones. The air here was different—thick, heavy, and smelling of brine and ancient dust. It was the smell of the deep city, the rot beneath the polished brass of the upper districts. Behind her, Simeon adjusted his spectacles, the nervous twitch of his hand betraying the calm façade he tried to project. Katra brought up the rear, her hand resting on the hilt of the serrated cutlass at her hip, her eyes darting into the shadows. They expected chaos. They expected the guards to be in disarray, celebrating their leader’s capture or fighting over the spoils. They found only a tomb-like stillness. The underhold opened up into a vast, vaulted chamber that had once been a cistern. Rusted pipes, thick as a man’s waist, snaked along the ceiling, weeping a slow, rhythmic *drip-drip-drip* into puddles on the floor. In the center of the room, resting on a pedestal of black marble, sat the Scythan artifact—a jagged shard of metal that seemed to drink the light from the gas lamps. But the pedestal was not unguarded. Seated in a high-backed leather chair, positioned directly between the trio and the artifact, was a woman. She wore a tailored coat of midnight velvet, her grey hair swept back severely from a face lined with the hard geometry of command. In her lap rested a heavy, single-shot flintlock pistol, the brass barrel gleaming with fresh oil. It was Vorna. The Guild Master. Supposedly rotting in a cell at the Ironworks precinct. " You move quietly for aristocracy, little mouse," Vorna said. Her voice was like gravel grinding under a boot heel. "But not quiet enough." Aya froze. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic bird trapped in a cage of whale bone and corsetry. From the shadows of the archways, four figures emerged. They were Vorna’s personal retinue—brutes in heavy leather aprons, armed with steam-carbine rifles that hissed softly, pressure valves glowing a menacing orange. "Run," Simeon whispered, his voice trembling. "Don't," Vorna commanded, raising the pistol lazily. She didn't even aim it; she simply pointed it in their general direction, a gesture of absolute confidence. "The triggers on the carbines are hair-sensitive. Move a muscle, and you’ll be mist before you hit the floor." Katra snarled, stepping in front of Aya. "The raid... the coppers..." "Stagecraft," Vorna sneered. "A necessary culling of the ranks. I needed to know who was loyal and who was a rat. And look what the tide washed in." Two of the brutes rushed them. There was no time for Aya to reach for the Immateria, no time to whisper the syllables that would tear the veil. A rifle butt struck Katra in the shoulder, sending her sprawling. Simeon was seized by his collar, his pocket watch chain snapping as he was thrown to his knees. Aya tried to twist away, but a massive hand clamped over her mouth and arm, wrenching her backward. She struggled, kicking out, but the grip was iron. "The mask," Vorna said, rising from her chair. The pistol remained at her side. "Remove it." The brute holding Aya tore the black silk domino from her face. The silence that followed was heavier than the stone above them. Vorna stepped closer, her boots clicking rhythmically. She stopped inches from Aya, her eyes narrowing as she studied the pale skin, the high cheekbones, the terrified, defiant eyes of Lady Aya Vane. "Well," Vorna breathed, a cruel smile touching her lips. "The prodigal daughter of the Vane estate. The girl who reads thermodynamics in the parlor and sneaks out to bleed with the dregs of Morska." Aya’s blood ran cold. It wasn't just the threat of death; it was the threat of *revelation*. If her father knew... if the *Magistrate* knew what she was doing down here... the shame would destroy her family. And if they discovered *how* she had risen through the ranks—that she possessed the same cursed gift that had sent her mother to the gallows—Aya wouldn't just hang. She would be dissected in the name of science. "You look surprised, my lady," Vorna mocked, reaching out to tap Aya’s cheek with a gloved finger. "Did you think your little masquerade fooled me? I’ve known for months. I let you play your games because you were useful. But this... this is treason against your own guild." "We didn't know," Aya lied, her voice shaking but finding its footing. She needed to pivot. She needed leverage. "We heard you were taken. We came to secure the asset. To keep it from the Enforcers." "Save your breath for the noose," Vorna spat. She turned away, gesturing to the artifact. "Bind them. We’ll dump them in the harbor. The crabs don't care about titles." "No!" Aya shouted as the ropes bit into her wrists. Panic flared, hot and sharp. "Vorna, listen to me! You can't kill me." Vorna paused, looking back over her shoulder with a bored expression. "And why is that? Because you bleed blue blood?" "Because I am worth more alive than dead!" Aya straightened her spine, forcing the aristocratic mask back onto her face, channeling every ounce of her upbringing. "You have the artifact, yes. But you don't know how to use it. It’s Scythan tech fused with Immateria resonance. Your mechanics will blow themselves up trying to crack it open." Vorna’s eyes flickered with interest. She signaled the brutes to hold their fire. "Go on." "My father," Aya said, the words tasting like ash. "Lord Vane sits on the Council of Industry. He has access to the Royal Vaults. He has the schematics for the resonance dampeners." "You would sell out your father?" Vorna laughed, a harsh, barking sound. "You are more like us than I thought." "I am offering you a trade," Aya said, ignoring the look of horror on Simeon’s face. "My life. My cooperation. In exchange for theirs." She nodded toward Katra and Simeon. "And if I just take what I want?" Vorna asked, stepping close again. "I have the artifact. I have you. I can ransom you to your father myself." "He won't pay a ransom to a criminal," Aya countered, her mind racing, playing the odds. "He will send the Royal Navy. They will burn this district to the ground to sanitize the shame. But if *I* return... if *I* bring him the 'dangerous artifact' I 'recovered' from the thieves... I can deliver whatever you need from the inside." It was a desperate gambit. Aya knew that if she went back to the surface with this story, she would be under her father’s lock and key for the rest of her life. She would lose her freedom, her connection to the Immateria, everything she had fought for. But if she didn't, she would lose her life in this damp, dark hole. Vorna stared at her, the silence stretching out, marked only by the *tick-tick-tick* of Simeon’s pocket watch, lying on the floor near his knee. "You have your mother’s eyes," Vorna said softly, the mention of the executed witch sending a shiver through Aya. "And her penchant for dangerous bargains." Vorna holstered her pistol. She looked at the artifact, then back to the three captives. "Untie the lady," Vorna ordered her men. "Keep the other two in chains. If she tries anything... if she even looks at the shadows too long... shoot the boy first." As the ropes fell away, Aya rubbed her wrists, looking at the cold, calculating face of the woman who held her life in her hands. She hadn't escaped; she had merely traded a quick death for a slow suffocation. "Welcome to the inner circle, Lady Vane," Vorna said, her smile sharp enough to cut glass. "Pray you are worth the price."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the complex prompt requirements. The author successfully establishes the gritty Victorian-gothic atmosphere through evocative descriptions like "iron door groaned open on hinges that had not been oiled since the reign of the previous Emperor" and "the smell of the deep city, the rot beneath the polished brass of the upper districts." The worldbuilding effectively contrasts mechanical/industrial elements with forbidden magic. The dialogue feels authentic to the setting, with Vorna's voice particularly well-crafted - her lines like "You move quietly for aristocracy, little mouse" and "Save your breath for the noose" carry appropriate menace and class awareness. The character voices remain distinct and consistent throughout. Aya's internal conflict is well-developed, showing her desperation as she realizes the implications of exposure extend beyond personal death to family shame and the revelation of her magical abilities. Her negotiation strategy feels believable given her aristocratic background and desperate circumstances. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the ominous quiet entrance through the revelation of Vorna's presence to the capture and negotiation. The author avoids the requested fight scene while maintaining dramatic tension. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "fake arrest" reveal and the hostage negotiation follow familiar patterns. The prose occasionally veers toward purple territory with phrases like "frantic bird trapped in a cage of whale bone and corsetry," though it generally maintains appropriate restraint. The ending strikes the right dark tone, with Aya trading immediate death for a different kind of imprisonment, which feels earned given the established power dynamics and her limited options. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 75.7
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The neon sign outside the bar buzzes with a dying insect’s persistence, casting a sickly pink glow over the rain-slicked alley. Inside, the air hangs thick with stale smoke and the sour tang of spilled beer. It’s the kind of place where questions aren’t asked and debts are settled in lead. Spike Spiegel sits at the far end of the bar, a half-empty glass of whiskey sweating beside his hand. His suit, a shade of blue that’s seen better days, is rumpled but still carries a certain style. He taps a cigarette against the bar top, the motion practiced, almost lazy. His mismatched eyes—one real, one a cold prosthetic—scan the room with a disinterest that fools no one who knows him. The bounty puck in his pocket vibrates again. A simple snatch-and-grab: a data courier with a stolen corporate drive. The payout is just enough to cover the *Bebop*’s fuel and maybe a decent meal for a week. Jet had sent the details with a sigh that carried through the comms. The door swings open, letting in a gust of humid night air and a woman who seems to carry the storm inside her. Revy doesn’t enter a room so much as she occupies it. Her cut-off shorts and black crop top are a stark contrast to the dingy surroundings, but they fit her like armor. The twin holsters at her hips, holding her beloved Cutlass pistols, are not concealed. They are a statement. The dragon tattoo winding up her right arm seems to writhe in the dim light as she strides to the bar, her boots thudding with authority. She slaps a crumpled bounty puck on the counter. "Whiskey. Neat. The bottle." The bartender, a man who looks like he’s been carved from old leather, doesn’t flinch. He just slides a bottle and a glass her way. Spike watches her in the mirror behind the bar. He recognizes the look. The coiled tension, the eyes that catalog exits and threats in a single sweep. She’s a professional. And her puck is the same make and model as his. *Whatever happens, happens,* he thinks, a faint smile touching his lips. Revy feels the gaze. She doesn’t turn. She pours a generous measure of whiskey, knocks it back, and pours another. "Take a picture, cowboy. It lasts longer." "Was just admiring the hardware," Spike says, his voice a low, easy drawl. He turns on his stool, leaning back against the bar. "Nice pair of Colts. You handle them okay with that grip?" Revy’s head snaps toward him, her eyes narrowing. The air crackles. "You wanna find out if I can handle them, pretty boy? Or are you just here to run your mouth while I do the real work?" Spike takes a slow drag from his cigarette, letting the smoke curl from his lips. "The courier. 5'10", brown coat, nervous twitch. He’s due here in ten minutes to make a drop. My bounty." A dangerous grin spreads across Revy’s face, showing teeth. It doesn’t reach her eyes. "Funny. My intel says the same guy, same time. And my client pays double what the Federation’s offering." "Corporate types," Spike says with a shrug. "Always throwing money around. Doesn’t change the fact he’s mine." "In your dreams, space cowboy." She takes another swig from the bottle, her eyes never leaving his. There’s a challenge there, but something else too. A flicker of recognition. They speak the same language—the language of gunpowder and bad choices. The door opens again. The courier. A weasel of a man, sweating profusely, clutching a briefcase to his chest like a life preserver. He sees them both. He freezes. Spike and Revy move in unison. It’s not a conscious decision; it’s instinct. Spike slides off his stool, a graceful, boneless motion. Revy pushes away from the bar, her hands hovering over her pistols. The courier bolts, scrambling toward the back exit. "After you," Spike says with a gallant wave of his hand. "Fuck you," Revy snarls, and she’s moving, a blur of motion. They crash through the kitchen, past a shouting cook, and into the alley. The rain has intensified, turning the narrow space into a slick, treacherous gauntlet. The courier is fast, fueled by panic. Spike’s longer legs give him an edge. He leaps over a dumpster, landing in a roll that brings him up just ahead of Revy. She doesn’t slow down. She fires a single shot from her right-hand gun, not at the courier, but at a rusty fire escape ladder above him. The bullet sparks off metal, and the ladder crashes down, blocking the path. The courier skids to a halt, trapped. Spike and Revy converge on him, stopping a few feet apart, their weapons now drawn. Spike’s Jericho 941 is steady in his hand. Revy’s twin Cutlasses are aimed, one at the courier, one loosely in Spike’s direction. "Drop the case," Revy barks, her voice raw. "Nice trick with the ladder," Spike says, not taking his eyes off the prize. The courier, trembling, looks between the two lethal figures. He makes a desperate choice and throws the briefcase at Spike, then tries to scramble up the blocked fire escape. In that split second, Revy’s focus shifts. Her left-hand gun tracks the courier. Spike’s hand shoots out, catching the case. He sees her finger tighten on the trigger. He doesn’t think. He moves. A sharp, precise kick from his Jeet Kune Do arsenal connects with her wrist, not hard enough to break bone, but enough to send the shot wide. The bullet sparks off the brick wall inches from the courier’s head. The courier freezes, paralyzed with fear. Revy whirls on Spike, her eyes blazing with pure, undiluted fury. "What the fuck was that?!" "He’s worth more alive," Spike says calmly, though his heart is pounding a little faster. The adrenaline, the rain, the sheer kinetic energy of her—it’s intoxicating. "And you were about to redecorate the alley with his brains." "He was running! That’s what runners get!" she screams, stepping closer, her gun now pointed squarely at his chest. The barrel is so close he could feel the heat from her last shot. Spike doesn’t raise his gun. He just looks at her, a strange, sad smile on his face. "You’re all flash and noise, Two Hands. You ever try just… catching them?" The use of her moniker stops her cold. The rain plasters her hair to her face, runs in rivulets down her tattooed arm. For a moment, the rage in her eyes is replaced by something vulnerable, something startled. A police siren wails in the distance, getting closer. "The heat’s coming," Spike says, his voice dropping. "My ship’s docked at Bay 12. There’s a motel across from it. The ‘Stardust.’ Room 7. We can… divvy up the bounty there. Or fight over it. Your call." He holds her gaze for a second longer, then turns and grabs the paralyzed courier by the collar. "You’re with me, pal." Revy stands in the rain, watching him go. Her guns are still raised, but the fight has gone out of her stance. She’s breathing hard, not just from the chase. With a curse that’s more breath than word, she holsters her weapons and follows. *** The motel room is a study in despair. A single buzzing fluorescent light illuminates stained walls and a bedspread that might have been floral in a previous life. Spike has the courier zip-tied to the radiator, looking miserable. The briefcase sits on the chipped formica table. Revy slams the door shut behind her, throwing the deadbolt. The sound is like a gunshot in the small space. She’s soaked, her clothes clinging to her. She stalks to the table, grabs the bottle of cheap whiskey left by a previous occupant, and drinks directly from it. Then she rounds on Spike, who is leaning against the wall, lighting a fresh cigarette. "Alright," she says, her voice a low growl. "Talk. How do we split this? And don’t give me any fifty-fifty bullshit. I had the shot." "You had a mess," Spike corrects, exhaling smoke. "I had a clean extraction. My client wants him intact. What does yours want? The drive? I’ll wipe it and give you the shell." "I want the full bounty, asshole!" Spike pushes off the wall. He moves slowly, deliberately, into the center of the room. The space feels suddenly much smaller. "You know," he says, his voice softer now, almost intimate, "for someone who’s ‘just here,’ you sure fight hard for things that don’t matter." Revy’s breath hitches. He’s too close. She can smell the rain on his cheap suit, the tobacco, the faint scent of something metallic—gun oil. Her hand drifts toward her holster. Spike’s prosthetic eye seems to gleam in the dim light. "Whatever happens, happens, right?" he murmurs. His hand comes up, not toward his gun, but toward her face, as if to brush a wet strand of hair from her cheek. It’s the wrong move. In a flash, Revy’s gun is out, the barrel pressed hard under his chin, tilting his head up. Simultaneously, Spike’s Jericho is free, its muzzle resting against her ribs, just below her heart. They stand frozen, locked in a deadly embrace. Her gun under his chin, his gun at her side. Their faces are inches apart. Her eyes are wide, pupils dilated. His are unreadable, one dark and deep, the other a flat, glassy blue. The only sounds are their ragged breaths, the drip of water from their clothes onto the floor, and the steady buzz of the light. "Bad habit," Spike whispers, his lips barely moving against the cold steel of her gun. "Trusting people." "You have no idea," Revy breathes back, her voice trembling with rage, adrenaline, and something else she refuses to name. His thumb brushes against her side, where the gun rests. It’s not a disarm. It’s a touch. A question. She doesn’t pull the trigger. He doesn’t pull the trigger. The standoff holds, a perfect, terrible equilibrium of violence and want. The bounty, the courier, the money—it all fades into the buzzing fluorescent hum. All that exists is the pressure of gunmetal against skin, the heat of their bodies in the cold room, and the unspoken, unresolved truth hanging between them like the smoke from his cigarette. Whatever happens, happens. And right now, nothing is happening, and everything is.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding. The author successfully captures both Spike's laid-back fatalism and Revy's volatile aggression while maintaining their distinct voices throughout. The noir atmosphere is well-established through vivid environmental details like the buzzing neon sign and rain-slicked streets. The story structure follows a classic escalation pattern from initial meeting to chase sequence to final confrontation. The pacing is generally well-controlled, building tension effectively toward the motel room standoff. The author shows good understanding of both source materials, incorporating character-specific details like Spike's prosthetic eye, Jeet Kune Do skills, and Revy's twin pistols and dragon tattoo. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters, with Spike's casual drawl contrasting nicely against Revy's profanity-laced aggression. The sexual tension builds naturally through their competitive dynamic and mutual recognition of each other's skills. However, the piece suffers from some overwrought moments, particularly in the final standoff where the prose becomes somewhat purple ("perfect, terrible equilibrium of violence and want"). Some descriptions veer toward the melodramatic, and certain metaphors feel forced. The ending, while atmospheric, relies heavily on unresolved tension without providing much narrative satisfaction. The action sequences are competently written but not particularly innovative. The chase scene follows predictable beats, and the resolution feels somewhat anticlimactic. While the character work is solid, the plot itself offers few surprises beyond the basic premise. The piece succeeds in creating the requested atmosphere and sexual tension while staying true to both characters, but it doesn't transcend its genre conventions or offer particularly fresh insights into either character. + Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 80.9
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The pine trees never truly went silent in Norrland. Even in the deepest freeze of a January midnight, there was a sound—a dry, rasping whisper, like paper tearing in the next room. I remember that sound more clearly than the cold, though the cold was the thing that eventually drove us down. It was the summer of 1995. I had been ripped from the concrete pulse of Stockholm and transplanted into the endless, suffocating green of the north. I hated it. I hated the silence, the way the sun refused to set, and the way the villagers looked at me with polite, pitying eyes. Astrid was the only thing that made the exile bearable. She was local, her family tree rooted so deep in the village soil that she claimed her grandmother still spoke to the *vættar*—the spirits of the place. To Astrid, the ancient ways weren't history; they were a toolkit. We had spent the summer testing her grandmother’s "receipts," as she called them. Little midnight games to see who was lying about their crush, or to sour the milk of a teacher who had given us detention. It felt like rebellion. It felt like power. Tonight, however, was different. Tonight was *Útiseta*. "Sitting out," Astrid translated, her breath fogging in the air as she checked her watch. "You sit on the earth, and you don't move until the earth speaks back." We were standing at the edge of the old cemetery, the one that predated the church by a thousand years. In the midsummer twilight, the landscape looked bruised—shades of violet and deep blue. Astrid had insisted on the preparations with a matter-of-fact seriousness that unnerved me. No phones. No watches (except hers, hidden in her pocket). We had to walk backward from the main road until we hit the tree line, a trick she said confused the spirits so they couldn't follow us home if we failed. "You have to empty your pockets," Astrid instructed, her voice dropping to a whisper. "Nothing metal. Iron hurts them." I emptied my pockets onto a flat rock. A few kronor, a house key, a tube of lip balm. I felt stripped, vulnerable. "And you can't speak. Not a word. If you speak, you invite something else in. Something that mimics." She led me up the slope to the mound. It wasn't a grave in the modern sense, just a rise in the earth, covered in thick, spongy moss that felt like skin under my boots. A single, gnarled pine tree stood at the summit, its branches twisted into shapes that looked like arthritic fingers grasping for the sky. We sat. The instructions were simple: cross your legs, keep your spine straight, and stare at the horizon. Do not look at the tree behind you. For the first twenty minutes, it was a joke. My legs went numb. The mosquitoes were relentless. I wanted to giggle, to break the tension, to ask Astrid if this was just an elaborate way to make me sit on a bug-infested hill. But I remembered her warning about mimics, and I held my tongue. Then, the temperature dropped. It wasn't a gradual cooling. It was as if someone had opened a door to a deep freezer right beside me. The wind picked up, rustling the pine needles. *Shhh-shhh-shhh.* It sounded like a frantic, dry whisper. *Don't look at the tree,* I told myself. I focused on the horizon, where the sun was a thin, angry red line refusing to die. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic rhythm that seemed too loud. I felt a sensation of weight pressing down on my shoulders—not heavy, but deliberate. Like a hand resting there. I glanced at Astrid. She was rigid, her eyes wide, staring straight ahead. She wasn't acting. I could see the goosebumps on her arms even in the dim light. The wind stopped. The silence that followed was heavier than the noise. It pressed against my eardrums. I had the sudden, violent urge to turn around. The feeling wasn't curiosity; it was a primal, screaming command from my brain stem. *Something is standing right behind you.* I fought it. I dug my fingernails into my palms. *It’s just the power of suggestion,* I told myself. *It’s the isolation. It’s the cold.* Then I heard it. It wasn't the wind. It was a sound like heavy, wet canvas dragging across the moss. *Shhhhff. Shhhhff.* It was coming from the tree. I looked at Astrid. Her eyes were filling with tears, but she didn't move. She was staring at a point just over my shoulder. She saw something. Or she thought she did. The dragging sound stopped, replaced by a low, rhythmic creaking. Like a branch straining under a heavy load. The air smelled suddenly of ozone and damp root cellars. I realized then that we had crossed a line. We had treated this like a game, a teenage thrill to pass the long, bright nights. But the thing behind me didn't feel like a game. It felt ancient, and it felt hungry. Astrid’s grandmother’s recipes weren't just folklore; they were warnings. A cold draft touched the back of my neck, distinct and localized, like breath. I broke. I scrambled up, my boots slipping on the moss. "I'm done," I gasped, the words tearing the silence. "Astrid, I'm done." Astrid didn't move for a second. Then, she scrambled up too, her face pale. She didn't scold me for speaking. She just grabbed my arm, her grip bruising, and yanked me down the slope. We didn't walk backward. We ran. We ran until our lungs burned and the mound was lost in the darkness behind us. We never spoke of what Astrid saw over my shoulder. We never played the midnight games again. I moved back to Stockholm the following autumn. I told myself it was because I missed the city, because the north was boring. But even now, decades later, when the wind rustles the leaves in the park, I feel that cold draft on my neck. And I wonder, with a chill that has nothing to do with the weather, if we ever really left that mound, or if we just learned to ignore the thing that followed us down.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing the folk horror atmosphere of rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. The author effectively employs the first-person retrospective narrative voice, creating an authentic sense of adult Linnea looking back on a formative teenage experience. The writing maintains the crucial ambiguity between supernatural and psychological explanations for the events, which is essential to effective folk horror. The characterization is well-executed, particularly in establishing the dynamic between Linnea and Astrid. Linnea's reluctance and growing unease feel genuine, while Astrid's matter-of-fact approach to ancient practices creates an effective contrast. The author successfully portrays teenage behavior—the mix of rebellion, curiosity, and bravado that leads to poor decisions. The prose quality is generally strong, with evocative imagery that builds atmosphere effectively. Descriptions like "the landscape looked bruised—shades of violet and deep blue" and "moss that felt like skin under my boots" create visceral, unsettling imagery. The sensory details—the sound of pine needles, the sudden temperature drop, the smell of ozone—work well to build tension. The pacing is well-controlled, building from mundane preparations through mounting unease to genuine terror. The author effectively uses restraint, never fully revealing what Astrid sees, which maintains the story's ambiguity and enhances its unsettling quality. However, there are some weaknesses. Certain phrases feel slightly overwrought ("concrete pulse of Stockholm," "suffocating green of the north"), and some descriptions border on purple prose. The ending, while atmospheric, relies somewhat heavily on familiar horror tropes about things that "follow you home." The piece occasionally tells rather than shows, particularly in explaining the characters' backgrounds and motivations. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and age-appropriate. The author successfully avoids the trap of making teenagers sound too adult or too juvenile. The technical aspects of the writing—sentence flow, coherence, and structure—are solid throughout.
